data.title: Australia's trade surplus narrows in April to AUD 5.41B on weaker exports, higher imports
data.description:
Australia’s trade surplus narrowed to AUD 5.41 billion in April, below expectations and down from a revised AUD 6.89 billion in March and below market expectations of AUD 5.90 billion. The decline was driven by a 2.4% fall in exports and a 1.1% rise in imports. 

Australia's goods exports dropped 2.4% month-over-month to AUD 44.08 billion in April 2025, reversing a downwardly revised 7.2% rise in March, as shipments to the US were dampened by Trump's tariffs. 

Australia’s goods imports rose 1.1% month-over-month to AUD 38.66 billion in April 2025, rebounding from a revised 2.4% decline in March, partly due to U.S. President Trump’s 90-day suspension of reciprocal tariffs. 

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose 0.3% to above 8,560 on Thursday, extending its rally to a third consecutive session. The Australian dollar held its recent gains around $0.649 on Thursday.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWA), (NYSEARCA:FXA), (NYSEARCA:FLAU).

More on Australia

  • Australia's Q1 GDP misses estimates amid weak public spending, consumer demand, and exports

  • RBA Minutes: Rate cut signals caution, data to drive future policy

  • Australia inflation gauge eases slightly in April by 0.6%

  • Australia's trade surplus shrinks to 2020 low; imports surge, exports fall

  • Australia’s monthly CPI eased to three-month low of 2.4% in February

data.title: China's services sector growth accelerates in May amid stronger business & activity
data.description:
The Caixin China General Services PMI rose to 51.1 in May 2025, up from April’s seven-month low of 50.7 and in line with market forecasts, supported by faster growth in new business and activity, despite a renewed decline in new export orders due to uncertainty over US tariffs. 

The Caixin China General Composite PMI dropped to 49.6 in May 2025 from 51.1 in the previous month, signaling the first contraction in private sector activity since December 2022.

“Downward pressure on the economy has significantly intensified compared to preceding periods,” said Dr. Wang Zhe, Senior Economist at Caixin Insight Group. He added that the effectiveness of previous consumption-stimulating measures needs further evaluation, and follow-up policies should be tailored to current economic conditions.

The Shanghai Composite were little changed on Thursday, struggling for clear direction amid heightened uncertainty on Sino-US trade relations.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:FXI), (NYSEARCA:KWEB), (NYSEARCA:CQQQ), (NASDAQ:MCHI), (NYSEARCA:ASHR), (NYSEARCA:YINN), (NYSE:TDF), (NYSEARCA:CHIQ), (NYSEARCA:GXC), (NYSEARCA:EWH), (NYSEARCA:KBA), (NYSEARCA:YANG), (NASDAQ:CXSE), (NYSE:CAF), (NYSEARCA:CWEB), (NASDAQ:PGJ), (NYSEARCA:KURE).

Currency: (CNY:USD)

More on China markets and economy

  • China’s manufacturing activity unexpectedly contracted in May amid weak output, new orders, and exports
  • China's consumer prices fall for third month; PPI fall the most in 6 months
  • China service growth hits 7-month low as trade disruptions weighs on new business orders
  • PBoC encourages Yuan use in global trade amid rising tensions
  • People’s Bank of China keeps LPR rate steady for 6th month

data.title: Kimberly-Clark nears $3.5B sale of international tissue business to Suzano - WSJ
data.description:
Kimberly-Clark (NASDAQ:KMB) is nearing a sale of its Kleenex and tissue businesses outside of North America to Brazilian pulp producer Suzano (NYSE:SUZ) for ~$3.5B, in a deal that could be completed as soon as tomorrow, The Wall Street Journal reported Wednesday.

The sale would be part of an attempt by the maker of Scott toilet paper and Huggies diapers to focus on more profitable areas, according to the report.

Kimberly-Clark's (NASDAQ:KMB) global tissue business, known as the International Family Care & Professional segment, is the smallest of the company's three core divisions, with annual net sales totaling less than $3.5B and the lowest profit margins of the three.

A sale would leave Kimberly-Clark (KMB) with its North America business, the company's largest, with ~$11B in net sales last year, as well as its international personal care division, whose net sales amounted to ~$5.7B in 2024.

Any deal would provide big boost in the global tissue market for Suzano (NYSE:SUZ), which acquired Kimberly-Clark's (KMB) tissue business in Brazil in 2023, propelling its overall tissue market share.

data.title: MongoDB raises FY26 revenue guidance to $2.29B as Atlas customer growth accelerates, while expanding share repurchase program
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MongoDB (MDB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Dev C. Ittycheria stated, "We generated revenue of $549 million, a 22% year-over-year increase and above the high end of our guidance. Atlas revenue grew 26% year-over-year, representing 72% of revenue. We generated non-GAAP operating income of $87 million for a 16% non-GAAP operating margin, and we ended the quarter with over 57,100 customers." He highlighted, "Total customer net adds are the highest in over 6 years, reflecting the continued strong adoption of MongoDB across a wide range of industries and use cases." Ittycheria also emphasized, "We are beginning to see the benefit of our decision to focus our resources on the high end of the market where we have the largest opportunity."
  • He cited the acquisition of Voyage AI and the release of Voyage 3.5 as key advances, asserting, "By combining these into one platform, we make it dramatically easier for developers to build intelligence responsive apps without stitching together multiple systems."
  • Strategic priorities include moving upmarket, modernizing legacy applications, and investing in operational efficiency. New leadership appointments were announced: Mike Berry as CFO and May Petry as CMO.
  • CFO Michael J. Berry remarked, "Driving profitable growth with operational excellence and discipline is a priority for the whole leadership team." Berry introduced an expansion of the share repurchase program: "Our Board of Directors has authorized an increase to our share repurchase program, under which we may repurchase up to an additional $800 million of our common stock... bringing the total authorization to $1 billion."

Outlook

  • Berry stated, "We are raising our full year revenue guidance by $10 million, which reflects the continued confidence in Atlas while incorporating some timing differences in our EA business."
  • He added, "We are raising our expectations for operating margin to 12% at the midpoint, up from 10% in our initial fiscal year guidance."
  • For Q2, revenue is expected to be in the range of $548 million to $553 million, non-GAAP income from operations in the range of $55 million to $59 million, and non-GAAP net income per share in the range of $0.62 to $0.66.
  • For FY26, revenue is expected to be in the range of $2.25 billion to $2.29 billion, non-GAAP income from operations in the range of $267 million to $287 million, and non-GAAP net income per share in the range of $2.94 to $3.12.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q1 was $549 million. Atlas represented 72% of total revenue, up from 71% last quarter. Customer base grew by approximately 2,600 sequentially to over 57,100, with Atlas customers at over 55,800.
  • Gross profit was $407 million, with a gross margin of 74%. Operating income reached $87 million, and net income was $86 million or $1 per share.
  • The company ended the quarter with $2.5 billion in cash, operating cash flow of $110 million, and free cash flow of $106 million.
  • Non-Atlas revenue came in ahead of expectations, while net ARR expansion rate was approximately 119%.

Q&A

  • Sanjit Kumar Singh, Morgan Stanley, asked about Atlas growth drivers. Ittycheria explained MongoDB supports a wide variety of use cases across transactional and modern applications, and strong customer additions indicate robust adoption.
  • Raimo Lenschow, Barclays, questioned competition from Postgres and recent moves by Snowflake and Databricks. Ittycheria stated, "Just because they're buying a small Postgres companies... it's not clear to me why the world needs a 15th or 16th Postgres derivative database... The real advantage is architecture."
  • Jason Noah Ader, William Blair, raised concerns about long-term relevance of relational databases. Ittycheria responded that MongoDB is optimized for modern, complex applications, particularly in AI, while Postgres popularity is driven by SQL market consolidation.
  • Kash Rangan, Goldman Sachs, inquired about growth initiatives. Ittycheria noted progress in R&D, upmarket moves, and awareness initiatives, with customer adoption of version 8.0 occurring twice as fast as the previous release.
  • Multiple analysts probed on Atlas consumption trends, with Berry indicating monthly consumption growth was strong early in the quarter, soft in April, and rebounded in May.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts showed a slightly positive tone, focusing on Atlas growth, competitive positioning against Postgres, and clarity on customer acquisition and consumption trends. Questions were direct but often acknowledged the strong Q1 performance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing architectural advantages, market positioning, and operational discipline. Berry used phrases such as, "we feel really good about the organic growth story here" and "we are confident."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone was more upbeat, with management highlighting improved operating margin guidance and strong customer growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Revenue rose slightly from $548.4 million in Q4 to $549 million in Q1. Atlas' share of revenue increased from 71% to 72%, and customer growth accelerated, with net adds reaching a six-year high.
  • Operating margin declined from 21% in Q4 to 16% in Q1, but full-year operating margin guidance was raised from 10% to 12%.
  • Management's tone shifted from cautious optimism to increased confidence, particularly around Atlas, upmarket focus, and new leadership. Analysts' tone became more positive, focusing on growth momentum and improved margin outlook.
  • Strategic focus remains on AI, application modernization, and self-serve customer acquisition, but investments are now paired with sharper focus on efficiency and profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic volatility affecting Atlas consumption, with softness in April followed by a rebound in May.
  • Berry noted a "$50 million headwind from multiyear license revenue in fiscal year '26, primarily impacting the second half of the year."
  • Uncertainty in the non-Atlas business persists, with expectations for high single-digit year-over-year decline.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of Atlas growth, the pace of AI adoption, and competitive threats from Postgres and hyperscalers.

Final Takeaway

MongoDB reported a strong start to fiscal 2026, marked by accelerating Atlas growth, record customer additions, and an increased focus on profitable expansion. The company raised its full-year revenue and operating margin guidance, reflecting confidence in its architectural leadership and ongoing investments in AI and application modernization. The newly expanded $1 billion share repurchase program underscores management’s commitment to shareholder value and long-term growth as MongoDB continues to position itself as a critical platform for next-generation applications.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Verint targets 8% ARR growth and double-digit free cash flow for FY26 driven by accelerating AI adoption
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Verint Systems Inc. (VRNT) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Dan Bodner, Chairman and CEO, opened by stating, "I'm pleased to report that we started the year with a strong first quarter and are on track to achieve our annual targets. In Q1, ARR growth accelerated to 6%, reflecting our continued AI momentum. And both revenue and diluted EPS came ahead of guidance." He emphasized Verint's differentiation through its ability to "transform the latest AI technology into strong tangible AI business outcomes delivering customer value better than any other CX vendor" and highlighted the company's hybrid cloud model for rapid AI deployment.
  • Bodner cited two major wins in Q1: a $13 million TCV order from a leading insurance company and a $14 million TCV order from a leading healthcare company. He pointed out that the healthcare customer nearly doubled its ARR from $8 million to $15.6 million in the past year through expanded AI adoption.
  • "We kicked off the year strong, expect our AI momentum to continue and are targeting exiting the year with 8% ARR growth and double-digit free cash flow growth," Bodner stated.
  • Grant Highlander, CFO, said, "ARR growth accelerated to 6% year-over-year, and we overachieved our revenue and non-GAAP diluted EPS guidance. Revenue came in at $208 million and non-GAAP diluted EPS came in at $0.29."
  • Highlander added, "Our rolling 4-quarter SaaS pipeline has increased more than 30% year-over-year, reflecting strong demand for AI."

Outlook

  • The company maintained its annual guidance, stating, "We are targeting $960 million of revenue with a range of plus/minus 3% driving non-GAAP diluted EPS of $2.93 at the midpoint." Highlander also projected, "For Q2, we expect ARR to increase to approximately $720 million, and we expect to exit the year with around $768 million of ARR, plus or minus 1%, reflecting 8% year-over-year growth."
  • Free cash flow is expected to increase approximately 12% to $145 million for the full year, driven by ARR growth and margin expansion.
  • Highlander said, "For the year, we expect AI ARR to continue to grow more than 20%, an acceleration from last year as customers increase usage and adopt additional bots."

Financial Results

  • Revenue was reported at $208 million for Q1, with non-GAAP diluted EPS of $0.29. Highlander indicated the revenue "overachievement was primarily due to the timing of two large, unbundled SaaS deals and therefore, do not impact our revenue and diluted EPS outlook for the year."
  • AI ARR increased 24% year-over-year, reaching $354 million, now representing close to 50% of subscription ARR.
  • Net debt stands at 1x last 12-month EBITDA, and Verint repurchased approximately 2.5 million shares during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Joshua Reilly, Needham: Asked about Verint's differentiation in the crowded AI space and the impact of chatbot adoption. Bodner responded that Verint stands out through "proven AI outcomes that are reported by leading brands" and by enabling "AI value now" with its hybrid cloud approach. He shared customer journeys demonstrating ARR growth from small initial deployments to large-scale adoption.
  • Reilly followed up on the impact of AI bots on seat renewals, to which Bodner said, "the success of the voicebots is very clear" and noted that while some customers are reducing agent numbers due to AI, the total number of agents managed remains at 4 million. He stressed, "50% of our ARR now is AI, and it's growing at 24%."
  • Shaul Eyal, TD Cowen: Asked about the AI adoption curve, Bodner replied, "it's the beginning of the customers actually willing to spend money," but cautioned, "I would not call it an inflection point at this point."
  • Peter Levine, Evercore: Sought confidence level in ARR ramp. Highlander responded, "the pipeline for our overall business, the SaaS ACV rolling fourth quarter, it's up greater than 30% year-over-year."
  • Levine asked about net retention on AI ARR. Highlander said, "the AI ARR is really the measure of our key driver of that growth...retentions are very high."
  • Levine also inquired about macro impact. Bodner explained, "each and every conversation with the customer is now focused on value and ROI that is measured in less than 6 months."
  • William Fitzsimmons, Jefferies: Asked about Verint's AI differentiation amid competition. Bodner emphasized Verint's platform, hybrid cloud, and open AI model, stating, "we are the only $1 billion pure play CX automation company."
  • Timothy Horan, Oppenheimer: Inquired about which bots are working best and customer reticence. Bodner described positive feedback for bots overall, with some adoption challenges tied to workforce behavioral change.
  • Horan followed up on working capital. Highlander said, "as the overall ARR and cash gen grows you will see the free cash flow continue to grow at a faster pace in double-digit periods."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on AI adoption, competitive differentiation, customer ROI, and sustainability of growth, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for potential risks and areas of execution.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing momentum and differentiation, and continued this confidence in Q&A, frequently citing "proven AI outcomes" and "strong pipeline." Slight caution emerged when discussing the pace of AI adoption, with phrases like "I would not call it an inflection point at this point."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained focused on tangible AI outcomes, while management's confidence was consistent, with a notable increase in emphasis on pipeline strength and AI-driven metrics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language was consistent, reiterating the $768 million ARR target and 8% growth, as in the prior quarter.
  • Strategic focus remained on scaling AI adoption and expanding through hybrid cloud deployments.
  • Analysts continued to focus on AI adoption rates, customer ROI, and differentiation, with ongoing attention to ARR sustainability and deal ramp-up.
  • Management's tone remained confident, highlighting acceleration in AI ARR growth (now at 24%) and a growing pipeline. Compared to the previous quarter, there was increased detail on specific customer expansion stories and the impact of large new deals.
  • Key financial metrics, such as revenue and EPS, were above guidance for the quarter, but management clarified that this resulted from timing, not a change in underlying trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged that the pace of AI adoption is still early-stage, with customers "very cautious" due to "noise and hikes that exist by many vendors" and negative experiences with other bots.
  • Customer adoption can be slowed by internal workforce behavioral changes and the need for unions or employee enablement for certain bots.
  • Management pointed to market caution, noting, "as much as we hear about the good stories, I think we hear less about some very bad stories with AI and that's what causing the market to be a little bit more cautious."
  • Mitigation strategies include focusing on "proven AI outcomes," scaling through hybrid cloud, and supporting customers with flexible, value-driven adoption models.

Final Takeaway

Verint began fiscal 2026 with accelerating AI-driven growth, reporting strong Q1 results in ARR, revenue, and EPS, and reaffirming guidance for 8% ARR growth and double-digit free cash flow for the year. Management highlighted continued momentum in AI ARR, now nearly half of subscription ARR, and pointed to a robust pipeline supported by large customer wins and expanding platform adoption. The company remains confident in its differentiation and ability to deliver tangible, rapid AI business outcomes, while acknowledging customer caution and the early stage of broader AI market adoption.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BARK signals accelerated revenue diversification and commerce growth to one-third of business amid tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BARK, Inc. (BARK) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Matt Meeker, CEO, emphasized three major takeaways: "First, we delivered our first ever adjusted EBITDA positive year. Second, we intend to remain adjusted EBITDA positive this year and beyond. And third, we plan to accelerate the diversification of our revenue faster than previously planned." Meeker detailed that Q4 adjusted EBITDA reached $5.2 million, marking the company's best quarterly result, and for the full year, BARK achieved $5.4 million in adjusted EBITDA. He explained the company pulled back on growth, citing "tariff-related uncertainty and potential downstream costs," and highlighted commerce business growth of 27% year-over-year to $68.3 million, with expanded retail partnerships. Gross margins hit a record 63.6% in Q4.
  • Meeker stated the company will "shift investment dollars more rapidly and aggressively in new product lines, distribution channels and services than previously planned," referencing the upcoming consumables line, new services from BARK Air, and acceleration into wholesale and e-commerce channels.
  • Zahir M. Ibrahim, CFO, said "Fiscal '25 was a significant year for BARK. We delivered our first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA, driven by ongoing margin expansion and improvements in operating efficiency."

Outlook

  • Ibrahim reported that, due to macro volatility and the evolving tariff landscape, the company is "unable to provide full year guidance at this time." For Q1, BARK expects total revenue between $99 million to $101 million, down 14% at the midpoint versus last year. Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 is projected between minus $1 million and $1 million.
  • Management highlighted that commerce is expected to constitute approximately one-third of the business over the next 2 to 3 years, up from 14% of total revenue in fiscal 2025.
  • Ibrahim explained, "By the end of fiscal 2026, we anticipate a more diversified and balanced sourcing footprint for our toy production."

Financial Results

  • BARK reported Q4 revenue of $115.4 million and full year revenue of $484.2 million, reflecting a 1.2% year-over-year decline. Commerce revenue reached $15.4 million in Q4 and $68.3 million for the year. BARK Air delivered $1.8 million in Q4 and nearly $6 million for the year. Consolidated gross margin rose to 63.6% in Q4. Total marketing expense in Q4 was $17.3 million.
  • The company ended the year with $94 million in cash, having spent $10.5 million to repurchase 6 million shares during the quarter and $18.5 million for 11.4 million shares in the full year. Inventory at year-end was $88 million.
  • G&A expenses were $114 million for the year, down $14 million. Shipping and fulfillment expenses were $139 million, nearly flat year-over-year.

Q&A

  • Maria Ripps, Canaccord, asked about supply chain diversification. Ibrahim responded, "We have the flexibility if we wanted to, to manufacture all of our toys outside of China by the end of this fiscal year."
  • Ripps inquired about the Shopify migration. Meeker said, "All of our active subscribers and new subscribers are coming through bark.co. All of our products are listed there...from a platform perspective, feeling really good about where we are with Shopify and how we're operating on it."
  • Ryan Meyers, Lake Street Capital, asked about D2C and commerce demand. Meeker explained the pullback in D2C marketing was due to tariffs and consumer sentiment, noting, "It's long overdue for us to really, really take this seriously and stop pouring every marketing dollar...into a toy business that has those dynamics and really isn't...the growth engine of the future."
  • Meyers also asked about commerce demand. Ibrahim said, "Commerce demand has been pretty strong. We grew 27% in fiscal '25...we expect Commerce to grow at a similar sort of level as what we saw in fiscal '25."
  • Kaumil Gajrawala, Jefferies, pressed on pricing and marketing. Meeker stated, "We're not fooling ourselves saying they were buying a $30 Barkbox, but now they'll pay $60...That's just not tenable."
  • Gajrawala asked about share buybacks. Ibrahim said, "We've been pretty aggressive about it through the quarter...we want that dry powder available to us as the year goes on."
  • Unidentified Analyst (Citigroup) asked about commerce segment pullbacks. Ibrahim said, "As we've started to see the tariffs come down...you're seeing that demand and the order placement coming back in."
  • The analyst also asked about the toy and consumables mix. Ibrahim clarified, "It's more heavily skewed today towards toys. I would say it's at least 90% is toys."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked probing questions about supply chain risk, revenue diversification, and marketing strategy, indicating a slightly cautious tone, especially regarding tariff exposure and D2C performance.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone, emphasizing adaptability and financial discipline. Phrases such as "we have a plan and are committed to achieving this" and "we know how to do it, and we have a plan" signaled confidence. During Q&A, management acknowledged challenges but reiterated a focus on maintaining EBITDA positivity and accelerating revenue diversification.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there was a noticeable shift from optimism about revenue growth and D2C momentum to a more defensive posture, especially in response to tariff and consumer sentiment concerns.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The previous quarter featured a confident outlook on top-line growth, D2C stabilization, and commerce expansion, as well as a reaffirmation of revenue and EBITDA guidance. In Q4, management withdrew full-year guidance due to macro and tariff uncertainties.
  • The strategic focus has shifted from scaling D2C and building on Shopify momentum to accelerating diversification into new product lines and channels, as well as mitigating concentrated exposure to tariffs.
  • Commerce growth remained strong but was slightly tempered by tariff-related order timing. Management's tone moved from forward-looking optimism to a more cautious, resilient stance, while analysts' focus shifted toward risk mitigation and sustainability of profitability.
  • Key metrics such as revenue and gross margin saw modest changes, but the most notable development was the company's first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited tariffs on Chinese imports—up to 145% at times—as a key risk, with efforts to diversify manufacturing and source outside China underway. Ibrahim pointed out, "Supplier transitions of magnitude carry risk and many key variables such as future tariff actions, trading policy, inflation and consumer response remain outside of our control."
  • There is macroeconomic uncertainty impacting consumer sentiment and D2C performance. Management is also contending with USPS rate changes.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of D2C acquisition, the pace of commerce growth, and the company's ability to offset tariff impacts without sacrificing margins or customer retention.

Final Takeaway

BARK, Inc. marked its first full year of positive adjusted EBITDA while navigating significant tariff headwinds and softening consumer sentiment. Management aims to sustain profitability and is accelerating diversification away from D2C subscriptions to commerce, new products, and innovative services like BARK Air. The company is proactively adjusting its supply chain to mitigate tariff risk, investing in new growth channels, and maintaining disciplined cost control. While the near-term outlook is clouded by macro and policy uncertainty, BARK enters fiscal 2026 with a strengthened financial foundation and a clear strategy to drive revenue diversification and resilience.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Argan signals backlog to surpass $2B in 2025 amid power sector momentum
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Argan, Inc. (AGX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO David Hibbert Watson highlighted a strong start to fiscal 2026, noting "consolidated revenue growth of 23% to $193.7 million and gross margin of 19%, led by continued momentum in our Power Industry Services segment." Watson also reported "record backlog of $1.9 billion as of April 30, 2025," reflecting full notice to proceed on the Sandow Lakes Energy Company project for a 1.2-gigawatt natural gas-fired plant in Texas. He emphasized, "Power demand has reached its highest level in 2 decades and is expected to increase further with the development of AI data centers, the onshoring of complex manufacturing, and the growing adoption of electric vehicles."
  • Watson stated the company is "energized about the demand environment, which we believe will present attractive project opportunities for the next decade and beyond."
  • The balance sheet remains strong with $546.5 million of cash and investments, net liquidity of $315 million, and no debt. Watson also noted an increase in the size of the share repurchase program to $150 million.
  • Watson announced a management change: "Sean Terrell, who has served as President of TRC since 2023, was recently appointed to the additional role of Chief Executive Officer. This change came as Bobby Foister, Jr., stepped down to take a reduced role as part of a longstanding succession plan."
  • CFO Joshua S. Baugher stated, "First quarter revenues increased 23% to $193.7 million, primarily reflecting strong revenue growth in our Power Industry Services segment as compared to the first quarter of fiscal 2025."
  • Baugher continued, "For the 3-month period ended April 30, 2025, Argan reported consolidated gross profit of approximately $36.9 million, or gross margin of 19%."

Outlook

  • Watson projected continued backlog growth, stating, "In the short-to-medium term, we expect to add several power industrial jobs over the course of the next 6 months, which should put us significantly over $2 billion in backlog later this year."
  • He further shared, "In the long term, we believe demand will remain strong for the next decade and beyond, and this is underscored by the fact that the OEMs are starting to fill 2030 gas turbine slots as they are primarily sold out of earlier years."
  • For the Industrial Construction Services segment, Watson commented, "TRC's backlog increased to $91 million, and our confidence in this segment is really strong. Based on current visibility, we expect revenues to increase meaningfully over the next several quarters."

Financial Results

  • Baugher reported consolidated gross profit of $36.9 million, or gross margin of 19%, for the first quarter of fiscal 2026. Net income was $22.6 million, or $1.60 per diluted share. EBITDA for the quarter was $30.3 million, or 15.6% as a percent of revenue.
  • The Power Industry Services segment generated revenues of $160 million, representing 83% of first quarter revenues and pretax book income of approximately $31 million.
  • Industrial Construction Services contributed $29 million in revenue and pretax book income of approximately $2 million, with its segment gross margin at 10.8%.
  • Telecommunications Infrastructure Services contributed 2% of first quarter revenues, with a segment gross margin of 18%.
  • The company returned $18 million of capital to shareholders during the quarter, including a quarterly dividend of $0.375 and share repurchases or net settlements of approximately 100,000 shares for $12.9 million.

Q&A

  • Robert Duncan Brown, Lake Street Capital: "What's sort of the pipeline look like for the rest of the year? And could you give us some color on the size and potential there?" Watson responded, "The pipeline remains strong, and we're very bullish on being able to continue to add to the backlog... we expect to add several power industrial jobs over the course of the next 6 months, which should put us significantly over $2 billion in backlog later this year."
  • Brown: "What's sort of the potential that backlog can get to given your capacity and given sort of what you see in the project pipeline?" Watson replied, "That's the guidance, right? It's significantly over $2 billion. We have project capacity in that 10-plus range between renewable and gas jobs."
  • Brown: "On the Industrial business, I think this quarter was sort of bottoming. What's the outlook there?" Watson answered, "We expected a slight contraction in this past quarter, which there was, but we're still seeing strong interest in TRC... we expect revenues to increase meaningfully over the next several quarters."
  • Christopher Paul Moore, CJS Securities: "Gross margins... can you quantify or even estimate the amount of excess margin in there from projects like Trumbull?" Watson said, "They basically reflect continued strong execution across the business, as well as the continued changing mix of projects and contract types... we expect to exceed last year's margin profile as we move through the year."
  • Moore: "Is there an optimal backlog level if most of it is natural gas?" Watson explained, "Our backlog bounces around some given that overnight, we could add $600 million, $700 million, $500 million contract... the trajectory of backlog [is] to increase over the course of the year, though it could bounce around some."
  • Moore: "Is the 3- to 4-year project timeline permanent?" Watson replied, "It's primarily supply chain driven... it is currently a 3- to 4-year timeline typically, though smaller jobs could be shorter."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on backlog growth, margins, and industrial segment recovery, with a positive tone and congratulatory remarks, such as "Congratulations on a good quarter."
  • Management conveyed confidence and optimism, repeatedly highlighting robust demand, a strong pipeline, and financial discipline. Watson used phrases like "we're very bullish" and "we remain very optimistic about our continued growth."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management maintained a positive tone, with continued focus on backlog, margins, and multi-year growth visibility. Management's confidence appeared to increase, using more assertive language about growth potential and project pipeline.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Backlog increased from $1.4 billion at January 31, 2025, to $1.9 billion at April 30, 2025, with management now guiding for backlog to move "significantly over $2 billion" later in the year.
  • Revenue for Q1 2026 was $193.7 million, down sequentially from $232.5 million in Q4 2025, reflecting seasonality and project timing, while net income and EBITDA also decreased from prior quarter levels.
  • Gross margin for Q1 2026 was 19%, compared to 20.5% in Q4 2025, but management indicated expectations to "exceed last year's margin profile as we move through the year."
  • The mix of backlog shifted toward natural gas projects, increasing to 67% versus 54% in the prior quarter.
  • The Industrial Construction Services segment saw a drop in revenue quarter-over-quarter but is expected to rebound in coming quarters.
  • Management tone grew more confident, especially regarding long-term demand and backlog growth, while analysts maintained a positive and inquisitive stance, focused on execution and margin sustainability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Watson addressed the variable timing of project starts, noting, "we often don't control the start times and new projects, so it's tough for us to give an exact estimate."
  • Project timelines for new gas jobs have extended from 2.5–3 years to 3–4 years, primarily due to supply chain issues.
  • Management highlighted the need to maintain execution discipline and manage risk effectively as project mix and volume increase.
  • Analysts probed for margin sustainability and the extent to which recent outperformance is repeatable, reflecting a cautious stance on future profitability.

Final Takeaway

Argan, Inc. began fiscal 2026 with considerable momentum, achieving substantial revenue and backlog growth driven by power sector demand, particularly for natural gas-fired facilities. Management guided for backlog to surpass $2 billion in the near term, supported by a strong project pipeline and robust market drivers such as AI, manufacturing onshoring, and electrification trends. The company remains focused on disciplined project execution, prudent capital allocation, and leveraging its capabilities for continued growth in energy infrastructure, positioning itself as a key partner in the sector’s multi-year expansion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Energy Transfer says U.S. to require licenses to export ethane to China
data.description:
Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) disclosed Wednesday it received a letter from the U.S. Commerce Department requiring a license to export ethane to China, and that it will file for an emergency authorization to continue exporting.

Another ethane exporter, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), said earlier on Wednesday it received notice from the department denying its emergency requests to export three proposed cargoes of ethane totaling 2.2M barrels to China.

Enterprise Products (EPD) had filed the emergency authorization requests after it received a letter requiring license authorization on May 23; Energy Transfer (NYSE:ET) said it obtained its letter on June 3.

The letters said exports of ethane pose an unacceptable risk of military end-use in China, according to filings from both companies.

The denial of Enterprise Products' (EPD) emergency requests calls into question whether it is just a short-term disruption, Vortexa's head of Americas analysis Samantha Hartke told Reuters.

"Near-term cargo reshuffles or resales could be necessary, as would a greater dependence on domestic storage," Hartke said, adding that short positions in ethane had mounted on Wednesday.
